response:
The differential equation provided combines logistic growth with a time-varying growth rate due to seasonal rainfall. The equation is:

\[ \frac{dP}{dt} = rP \left(1 - \frac{P}{K}\right) + A \sin\left(\frac{2\pi t}{12}\right)P \]

Substituting the given values \( r = 0.3 \), \( K = 1000 \), and \( A = 0.1 \):

\[ \frac{dP}{dt} = 0.3P \left(1 - \frac{P}{1000}\right) + 0.1 \sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{6}\right)P \]
\[ \frac{dP}{dt} = P \left[ 0.3 \left(1 - \frac{P}{1000}\right) + 0.1 \sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{6}\right) \right] \]
\[ \frac{dP}{dt} = P \left[ 0.3 + 0.1 \sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{6}\right) - \frac{0.3P}{1000} \right] \]

This is a Bernoulli differential equation of the form \( \frac{dP}{dt} + p(t)P = q(t)P^2 \), where:
\( p(t) = -0.3 - 0.1 \sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{6}\right) \)
\( q(t) = \frac{0.3}{1000} = 0.0003 \)

To solve this, we use the substitution \( u = P^{-1} \), which implies \( \frac{du}{dt} = -P^{-2} \frac{dP}{dt} \).
Dividing the original equation by \( P^2 \):
\[ \frac{1}{P^2} \frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{1}{P} \left[ 0.3 + 0.1 \sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{6}\right) \right] - 0.0003 \]
\[ -\frac{du}{dt} = u
